results.title: The poolExposure for token1 is erroneously calculated as shortPremium - longPremium;
results.reasoning: Yes. Audit item #1 explicitly flags the same computeNAV issue in PanopticVaultAccountant.sol: poolExposure1 is computed as longPremium.leftSlot() - shortPremium.leftSlot() instead of mirroring token0’s short - long. It’s the identical function/code path, mechanism (sign inversion), and impact (misstated NAV affecting fulfillDeposits/fulfillWithdrawals). The specific fix—compute poolExposure1 as shortPremium.leftSlot() - longPremium.leftSlot()—would resolve both descriptions.

results.title: NAV calculation inconsistency due to underlying token position in pool configuration
results.reasoning: Yes. The audit report explicitly flags the same computeNAV flaw in PanopticVaultAccountant: wallet/free balances of tokens (including the underlying) are mixed into per‑pool exposures and then clamped per pool, causing NAV distortions. Issue #11 (“Vault Free Balances Can Be Erased by Underwater Pool Positions”) describes how including the vault’s token balance inside the loop before the per‑pool zero‑clamp can wipe it out when that pool is underwater, which matches the ‘underlying included in pool’ side of the inconsistency. Issue #15 (“Per‑Pool NAV Clamping to Zero Inflates NAV by Ignoring Underwater Pool Debt”) highlights the root cause—per‑pool clamping instead of aggregating exposures and clamping once—and even notes distortions from mixing shared wallet balances into per‑pool exposure. This is the same mechanism the vulnerability description calls out (different NAV depending on whether the underlying token appears in the pool set, and whether it’s added inside the loop vs after it). The same fix—aggregate all exposures (including the underlying once) and apply Math.max at the end—would resolve both.
